1: yōshēb bĕsēter ʿelyôn bĕṣēl shadday yitlônān — whoever lives in the shelter of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty. Sēter (shelter, hiding place, secret place — from sātar: to hide) + ʿelyôn (Most High — the Canaanite epithet for the supreme deity, adopted as a title for YHWH). Bĕṣēl (in the shadow — the cool shade cast by a protecting presence) + shadday (the Almighty — the patriarchal name of God, especially prominent in Genesis and Job). The two divine titles (ʿelyôn and shadday) together invoke the God who is both supremely exalted and near enough to cast a sheltering shadow.
2: ʾōmar laYHWH maḥsî ûmĕṣûdātî ʾĕlōhay ʾebaṭaḥ-bô — I will say to the LORD, 'You are my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust.' Maḥseh (refuge — the place of shelter in storm or danger) + mĕṣûdāh (fortress, stronghold — the military fortification; from ṣûd: to hunt, to surround). The personal profession: ʾĕlōhay (my God) + ʾebaṭaḥ-bô (I trust in him) — the shift from third-person description (v1) to first-person confession, making the general promise personal.
3: kî hûʾ yaṣṣîlĕkhā mippah yāqûsh — he will rescue you from the hunter's trap. Naṣal (hiphil: to rescue, deliver — the Exodus deliverance verb) from the pah yāqûsh (fowler's snare — the trap laid for birds). The snare metaphor: the enemies and their plans as a fowler's trap into which the trusting person might fall but from which YHWH delivers. middeber hawwôt — and from every deadly disease. Deber (pestilence, plague — one of the Exodus plagues, here personified as a threat from which YHWH rescues).
4: bĕʾebrātô yāsekh lākh wĕtaḥat kĕnāphāyw teḥseh — he will shelter you under his feathers; you will find refuge beneath his wings. ʾEbrāh (feathers, pinions — the large flight feathers of a bird's wing) + kĕnāphāyw (his wings) — the dual metaphor of the mother bird protecting her young (cf. Deut 32:11's eagle metaphor; Ps 17:8; 36:7; Ruth 2:12). ṣinnāh wĕsōḥērāh ʾĕmûnātô — his faithfulness is your shield and armor. Ṣinnāh (large shield — the full body-shield) + sōḥērāh (encircling protection, buckler — a round shield or the protective encirclement itself). Divine faithfulness (ʾĕmûnāh) as body-armor — the reliability of God's character as the believer's defense.
5: lōʾ tîrāʾ mippahad lāylāh — you will not fear the terror that comes in the night. Pahad (dread, terror — the sudden overwhelming fear) that comes lāylāh (in the night — the time of darkness and vulnerability). mēḥēṣ yāʿûph yômām — nor the arrow that flies in the daylight. The four threats in vv5-6 span the dimensions of danger: night-terror, daylight-arrow (visible enemy), dark-pestilence, noon-destruction — every time and every kind of threat.
6: middeber bāʾōphel yahălōkh — nor the disease that moves through the dark. Deber (pestilence) that yahălōkh bāʾōphel (walks in darkness — the darkness of night). miqqeṭeb yāshûd ṣohŏrāyim — or the plague that strikes at noon. Qeṭeb (destruction, a destroying power — used in Deut 32:24 of pestilence; Isa 28:2 of storm-devastation) + ṣohŏrāyim (noon, midday — the blazing heat of noon). The qeṭeb ṣohŏrāyim became in the Septuagint the daimonion mesembrinon (the noonday demon) — the medieval spiritual tradition's identification of acedia (spiritual torpor) with this noon-striking plague.
7: yippōl mişşidĕkhā ʾelep ûrĕbābāh mîmînekhā — a thousand may fall beside you, ten thousand at your right hand. The military imagery: battlefield casualties surrounding the protected one. ʾElep (thousand) + rĕbābāh (ten thousand — the highest number word in Hebrew; cf. the song of 1 Sam 18:7). ʾēlekhā lōʾ yiggāsh — but it will not reach you. Nāgaʿ (to touch, reach, strike) — the destruction that strikes ten thousand will not make contact with the protected one. Not a claim of physical invincibility but of covenant protection amid universal mortality.
8: raq bĕʿênêkhā tabbîṭ — you will only watch with your own eyes. Raq (only, merely) — the sole action: watching with open eyes. wĕshillumāt rĕshāʿîm tirʾeh — and see the fate of the wicked. Shillumāh (retribution, recompense — from shālem: the paying back what is owed) of the wicked — the trusting person is a spectator to the judgment that falls on those who do not trust. The position is not active participation in the judgment but witness to it.
9: kî-ʾattāh YHWH maḥsî — because you have made the LORD your refuge. The kî (because) gives the reason for the protection of vv5-8: the ground is the decision made — YHWH chosen as refuge (maḥseh: the hiding place). ʿelyôn śamtā mĕʿônekhā — the Most High your dwelling place. Māʿôn — the same word as Ps 90:1 (God as the dwelling place) — here the human makes the divine dwelling place their own habitation. The symmetry: God is humanity's dwelling (Ps 90:1), and the Most High is the trusting person's dwelling (91:9).
10: lōʾ-tĕʾunneh ʾēlêkhā rāʿāh — no evil will happen to you. ʾĀnan (pual: to befall, happen to — used of bad things that meet you). wĕnega lōʾ-yiqrab bĕʾāhŏlekhā — and no plague will come close to your tent. Negaʿ (blow, plague, affliction) + bĕʾāhŏlekhā (your tent — the domestic, dwelling space). The protection extends from the person to the household. This is the protective covenant promise that undergirds the psalm — not a guarantee of absolute physical immunity but of the covenant shelter of divine care.
11: kî malʾākhāyw yĕṣawweh-lākh lishmorĕkhā bĕkhol-dĕrākhêkhā — for he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways. The divine commissioning of angels for personal protection — ṣāwāh (to command) applied to the angels' assignment. Bĕkhol-dĕrākhêkhā (in all your ways — not just the religious ways but every path). This verse and v12 are quoted by the adversary in Matt 4:6 / Luke 4:10-11 in the temple-pinnacle temptation — not a misquotation but a misapplication: the text promises protection in the normal paths of life, not in a spectacular act of self-endangerment to force God's hand.
12: ʿal-kappayim yiśśāʾûnekhā — they will carry you on their hands. Kappayim (palms of the hands) — the angels bearing the protected person in their open hands, preventing a fall. pen-tiggōph bāʾeben raglekhā — lest your foot strike against a stone. Nāgaph (to strike, stumble) — the protection from even ordinary stumbling. The extreme care described (angels carrying in their palms, preventing foot from touching a stone) expresses the totality of divine protection — it is this total protection that makes the adversary's misuse in Matt 4 a distortion: the promise covers the ordinary paths of life, not a theatric leap from the temple parapet.
13: ʿal-shaḥal wāphethen tidrokh — you will tread on the lion and the cobra. Shaḥal (young lion — the vigorous prime lion) + pethen (cobra, the Egyptian cobra — the most deadly serpent known to ancient Israelites). tirmos kĕphîr wĕtannîn — you will trample the young lion and the great serpent. Kĕphîr (young lion) + tannîn (sea dragon, great serpent — the chaos monster of Gen 1:21; Ps 74:13; Isa 51:9). The treading on dangerous creatures echoes Gen 3:15 (enmity between the seed and the serpent) and is echoed in Luke 10:19 (Jesus granting authority to tread on serpents). The dangerous creatures represent all powers hostile to the protected one.
14: kî bî ḥāshaq waʾăpalleṭēhû — 'Because he clings to me in love,' declares the LORD, 'I will rescue him'. The divine first-person speech (vv14-16) — God speaking directly about the trusting person. Ḥāshaq (to cling to, be attached to in love — the devoted attachment that cannot be separated; used of Israel's election, Deut 7:7). ʾăśaggĕbēhû kî yādaʿ shĕmî — I will set him beyond reach, because he truly knows who I am. Śāgab (piel: to set high and out of reach) — exaltation as protection. Yādaʿ shĕmî (he knows my name) — intimate personal knowledge of the divine name and character as the basis for the divine response.
15: yiqrāʾēnî wĕʾeʿĕnēhû — when he calls on me I will answer. The covenant prayer-promise: calling and answering as the basic transaction of relationship. ʿimmô-ʾānôkhî bĕṣārāh — I will be with him in his trouble. ʿImmô ʾānôkhî (I myself am with him) — the divine presence with the sufferer in the midst of the trouble (ṣārāh: distress, straits), not removal from it. ʾăḥallĕṣēhû waʾăkhabbĕdēhû — I will rescue him and give him honor. Ḥālaṣ (hiphil: to rescue, deliver — to pull free from danger) + kābēd (piel: to honor, give weight to — the bestowal of the divine honor on the one who clings to God).
16: ʾōrekh yāmîm ʾaśbîʿēhû — I will give him a long life. ʾōrekh yāmîm (length of days — the covenantal long life of Deut 11:9; Prov 3:16) — the divine gift of life's full measure. wĕʾarʾēhû bishûʿātî — and let him see my saving power. Rāʾāh bishuāʿāh (to see the salvation) — the final covenant gift: witnessing the divine salvation in its fullness. The psalm closes with God's own declaration — the divine voice directly promising what the entire psalm has described from the human side.
